coredata.dc:title: Structural and luminescent properties of CQDs produced by microwave and conventional hydrothermal methods using pelagic Sargassum as carbon source
coredata.dc:creator: Castañeda-Serna, Héctor U.
Calderón-Domínguez, Georgina
García-Bórquez, Arturo
Salgado-Cruz, Ma de la Paz
Farrera Rebollo, Reynold R.
coredata.dc:description: The synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from biomass could be the best application for biopolymeric materials with no further use in industry and that can have environmental effects when disposed of, such as pelagic Sargassum. This research compared the structural and luminescent properties of CQDs synthetized by two different methods (microwave and conventional hydrothermal synthesis) using pelagic Sargassum extract as a carbon source. The microwave method resulted in a better mass yield, while hydrothermal synthesis generated the best quantum yield (QY). Under optimized synthesis conditions, no statistical differences were observed in CQDs size or surface functional groups, as detected by TEM and FTIR analyses respectively. However, crystalline structure and light absorption varied between samples, explaining the higher values of QY observed in the conventional hydrothermal method. This study thus assists in furthering the understanding of the synthesis process, as well as providing supporting evidence for which synthesis methods are better for CDQ synthesis depending on the future uses of these nanoparticles.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Pelagic Sargassum
Luminescent properties
Microwave assisted synthesis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preconditioning of Sargassum and preparation of extract
Hydrothermal and microwave synthesis of CQD
Hydrothermal and microwave synthesis methods. Effect of process conditions
Characterization of CQDs synthesized at optimal conditions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: CQD's synthesis. Effect of process conditions and preparation method on mass yield: (A) microwave and (B) conventional hydrothermal.

CQD's synthesis conditions effect on QY response per method used: (A) microwave (B) conventional hydrothermal. Data from both synthesizing methods fitted to quadratic models with R2 values of 0.8108 for MS and 0.9288 for CHS. All dependent variables had significant effect (p < 0.0001) on the response.
TEM/HRTEM for each synthesis method (A) MS and (B) CHS, size distribution histogram for (C) MS and (D) CHS.
HRTEM for each synthesis method (A) MS and (B) CHS.
XRD pattern for each synthesis method. (A) microwave and (B) conventional hydrothermal synthesis.
FTIR spectra for each synthesis method (A) microwave and (B) conventional hydrothermal synthesis.
UV/Vis spectra for each synthesis method (A) microwave and (B) conventional hydrothermal synthesis.
CQDs fluorescence spectra, microwave synthesis (MS) and conventional hydrothermal synthesis (CHS).
- Factors and run for hydrothermal synthesis.
Comparison between responses for each synthesis.
TEM sizes intervals for CQDs synthetized by microwave and conventional heating.

head.ce:title: Structural and luminescent properties of CQDs produced by microwave and conventional hydrothermal methods using pelagic Sargassum as carbon source

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Departamento de Ingeniería Bioquímica, Escuela Nacional de Ciencias Biológicas, Instituto Politécnico Nacional, Mexico City, 07738, Mexico
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Departamento de Ingenieria Bioquimica, Escuela Nacional de Ciencias Biologicas, Instituto Politecnico Nacional, Mexico City, 07738, Mexico

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Departamento de Física, Escuela Superior de Física y Matemáticas, Instituto Politécnico Nacional, Mexico City, 07738, Mexico

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Escuela Superior de Física y Matemáticas

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Departamento de Fisica, Escuela Superior de Fisica y Matematicas, Instituto Politecnico Nacional, Mexico City, 07738, Mexico

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from biomass could be the best application for biopolymeric materials with no further use in industry and that can have environmental effects when disposed of, such as pelagic Sargassum. This research compared the structural and luminescent properties of CQDs synthetized by two different methods (microwave and conventional hydrothermal synthesis) using pelagic Sargassum extract as a carbon source. The microwave method resulted in a better mass yield, while hydrothermal synthesis generated the best quantum yield (QY). Under optimized synthesis conditions, no statistical differences were observed in CQDs size or surface functional groups, as detected by TEM and FTIR analyses respectively. However, crystalline structure and light absorption varied between samples, explaining the higher values of QY observed in the conventional hydrothermal method. This study thus assists in furthering the understanding of the synthesis process, as well as providing supporting evidence for which synthesis methods are better for CDQ synthesis depending on the future uses of these nanoparticles.

ce:section.ce:para: Seaweeds are a diverse group of photosynthetic and autotrophic organisms, with complex tissues and simple structures, classified into three categories according to their pigmentation: Chlorophyta (Green seaweeds), Rhodophyta (Red seaweeds), and Phaeophyta (Brown seaweeds), with this last group including more than 150 species [1,2]. These algae commonly live in the Sargasso Sea and in the northeast of the Gulf of Mexico, where they have been called pelagic Sargassum, with Sargassum fluitans and Sargassum natans being the most prevalent species [3]. Since the last decade, an unusual bloom of pelagic Sargassum has been observed in the Caribbean and African coasts, resulting in millions of tons of algae masses being deposited on regional shores [4]. Moreover, this phenomenon has shown an intense growth over the years even acquiring names such as “New Sargasso Sea” or “Great Atlantic Sargassum Belt” [5]. This phenomenon has affected the local fishing and tourism industries, as well as caused significant damage to the local ecosystems that could lead to a decrease in biodiversity, an increase in mortality of marine species, and a surge in disease in the population exposed to rotten Sargassum [4,6,7].

The chemical composition of Sargassum shows that the nutrients in the highe concentration are carbohydrates (18.97–67.92%), fiber (7.15–37.41%), and protein (2.99–15.4%) [7,8]. This composition suggests that it could be considered a good nutritional source for humans and animals, or a good material for use as a fertilizer. However, its mineral content also suggests that metals in its composition could affect soil salinity or be toxic upon consumption [6]. Nonetheless, a novel use of these algae could be the “green” synthesis of carbon nanoparticles such as Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs), which are considered materials of high economic value [9–12].

CQDs have received a lot of attention since 2004, when they were discovered by Xu et al. during the purification of carbon nanotubes [13]. They are defined as carbon particles within a size of 1–10 nm, formed by sheets of graphene or derivate graphite; the band-gap is influenced by the effects of quantum confinement, which has a high surface area-volume ratio, as well as sp2 hybridization of the crystalline core and a non-crystalline phase with surface functional groups [14–17]. They have extraordinary characteristics like good conductivity, wideband light absorption, low toxicity, high photoluminescence emission, good chemical stability, high compatibility with several polymeric, inorganic, organic, and biological materials, and good water solubility. Additionally, their synthesis is straightforward and low-cost [9,13–15,17–19].

Regarding photoluminescence emission, the CQDs size determines the fluorescence wavelength, regardless of chemical composition. As an example, it has been reported that particles with a size of 2 nm emit in blue, 2.3 nm in green, 3.5 nm in orange and 6 nm in red [13]. This fact has allowed CQDs to be used in multiple useful applications. They could be applied as fluorescent dyes due to their low toxicity, high quantum yield, good biocompatibility, and excellent fluorescent stability [10–12,20]; as sensors due to their affinity to other molecules like DNA, cytochrome c, nitric oxide and picric acid, and ions like Cu, Hg and Fe [9,20–25]; as component in solar cells, enhancing the short-circuit current density and external quantum efficiency [20]; as degradant of methylene blue, acid blue or acid red, by photocatalytic reactions; as reducer of silver ions, graphene oxide and carbon dioxide [26–28]; as well as adsorber of different molecules like H2S and CO2 [29]. However, CQDs could present low quantum yields when being synthesizing from biomass [21]. To overcome this problem, the nanoparticles could be doped with atoms such as nitrogen, sulfur, phosphorous, and others [16,18]. The molecules usually used for this purpose must possess amine groups, providing nitrogen for the reaction mixture, leading to the production of particles of high fluorescence and quantum yield [30].

The synthesis of CQDs can be carried out by two different approaches: top-down and bottom-up. The top-down approach consists of using microscopic molecules that break into nano-sized structures, using procedures such as laser ablation, chemical carbonization and arc discharge, among others [15,31]. In the bottom-up approach, nanoparticles are synthesized using simple molecules through polymerization and carbonization, with methods such as pyrolysis, electrochemical carbonization, and solvothermal and hydrothermal carbonization [9,15,31]. The latter is the most-often chosen for the synthesis of this type of particles due to its simplicity, since it is a one-step process with high production yields and follows the philosophies of green chemistry, as the solvent used is water, requires less energy, and uses biological waste as a raw material [15,32].

Carbonization is a spontaneous exothermic reaction, where the biomass is transformed into carbon-type residues, with low oxygen and hydrogen content. This process is carried out at temperatures lower than or equal to 300 °C, in the presence or absence of oxygen, with or without catalysts, and in a pressure range of 60–100 bar [32,33].

In the hydrothermal carbonization process, a carbon source is introduced into the water. Then, the heating process is carried out, followed by centrifugation, filtration, or dialysis [32]. The carbon source can be simple organic compounds (monosaccharides, disaccharides, amino acids, etc.) or complex organic compounds (polymers) [9,12,15,19].

The heating process can be achieved by conventional or microwave methods [33]. Conventional methods transfer heat by conduction and convection, without interactions between the substance and oxygen. The reaction is carried out in a special vessel under high-pressure conditions. Some disadvantages of this method include long residence times, surface heating, and less process control [34,35]. In the microwave heating method, the carbonization process is achieved in shorter times, moreover it has a selective, volumetric, and easy heating control [19]. CQDs have been reported to have different properties depending on the type of heating method used. The conventional heating process promotes materials with high quantum yield, small sizes but low mass yields; on the other hand, the microwave method provides better mass yields and uniform size distribution, but low quantum yields [15,19].

Although many reports that use both synthesis methods exist, most are focused on the synthesis of CQDs from simple compounds such as urea, citric acid, succinic acid, lysine, or ammonium citrate, without a comparison between different processes [18,29,36,37]. Application of the conventional heating hydrothermal method with waste or plant products as carbon sources has been scarcely reported, including materials such as Sargassum fluitans, red lentils, fish scales, grass, or banana peel waste [9,12,15,26]. Reporting of the microwave method coupled with the use of waste or plant products is even less [22,38]. A comparison between these methods has only been reported by Nallayagari et al. [19], who changed the carbon source depending on the applied procedure, and by Kasmiarno et al. [32] who used the same carbon sources (Rambutan and Pandan leaves), focusing on the optical properties of CQDs. The aim of our research is to study the effect of the heating method (conventional or microwave) on CQDs properties, using the extract of pelagic Sargassum as carbon source. This study could help improve the understanding the synthesis process, in addition to choosing the best method depending on the subsequent use of nanoparticles and their properties.

Pelagic Sargassum was manually collected in the summer of 2019 at Mahahual beach, Quintana Roo, México. An in-situ pretreatment was applied to the samples to allow transport to the laboratory. This consisted in washing the algae with seawater to remove most of the entrapped materials, followed by sun-drying for three days at room temperature (27 °C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preconditioning of Sargassum and preparation of extract
ce:section.ce:para: The Sargassum was conditioned following the methodology described by Godavarthi et al. [9]. Briefly, 50 g of dry Sargassum was placed in 2 L of tap water for 15 min with vigorous stirring (Thermo Scientific SP131325, USA). Then, the liquid was removed by passing it through a strainer (5 mm) eight times. This procedure was repeated three more times with distilled water.

The washed Sargassum was dried in a convection oven (AFOS Mini AK Kilin, England) at 35 °C for 24 h, and pulverized in a coffee grinder (Krups F408, France) for 2 min. Then, 10 g of dry Sargassum was placed in a beaker with 100 mL of distilled water and heated at 85 °C for 2 h. The mixture was filtered twice through a nylon mesh (250 μm) and once through Whatman No.1 filter paper, adapted with a Buchner funnel under vacuum conditions. The residual liquid (extract) was recovered and diluted in distilled water at a 1:6 ratio and refrigerated (4 °C) for later use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrothermal and microwave synthesis of CQD
ce:section.ce:para: Conventional hydrothermal synthesis was carried out according to Godavarthi et al. [9] in a 100 mL teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave (Baoshishan, Taiwan). In brief, 60 mL of extract was placed in the reactor and kept at a temperature range of 120–180 °C in an oven (Metrix, USA), with a reaction time of 120–720 min, both conditions were modified according to experimental design. After the reaction, the vessel was allowed to cool down at room temperature.

For the CQDs obtained by the microwave method, the Jeong et al. [20] procedure was followed, introducing 10 mL of extract in a 250 mL reaction glass flask, which was placed in a domestic microwave oven and irradiated at different power levels (200–800 J s-1) for a reaction time of 200–1200 s. The samples were obtained after natural cooling of the vessel.

In the next step, the purification process described by Vandarkuzahali et al. [39] was used. In both synthesis methods, the aqueous solution obtained was centrifuged (Velocity 18 R refrigerated centrifuge, Austria) at 5000 rpm (7166.66×g) for 15 min. After that, the sediment was removed, while the supernatant was mixed with acetone in a ratio of 2:1. To obtain a solution with fluorescent CQDs, the mixture was centrifuged at 15,000 rpm (21,500×g) for 15 min. As a final step the supernatant was oven-dried (Terlab TE-FH70DM, Mexico) at 35 °C for 24 h.

A central composite experimental design (Design Expert V 9.0, Stat-Ease In., MN, USA), centered on its faces, with 5 central point repetitions and duplicates in the other points, was used to study the change in quantum yield and mass yield for the two types of synthesis process. The levels of each variable (temperature and time reaction) were selected based on the information reported by Yang et al. [40] for both synthesis methods (CHS and MS) as shown in Table 1 . Response surface graphs, fitting model equations, determination coefficients and p-value of variables were reported.

The mass yield was calculated by the relationship between the concentrations of CQDs and the solids in the initial extract, using equation (1) reported by Xie et al. [41].
(1)
M a s s
y i e l d
( % )
=
C Q D s C
S E C
× 100

The nanoparticle concentration (CQDsC) and the Sargassum extract weight (SEC) were determined keeping the sample solution at 35 °C for 24 h. The samples were weighed in an analytical balance (Ohaus 220G PA224C, USA), and the concentration was calculated by the relationship between weight and volume used.

The quantum yield (QY) was determined as described by Xie et al. [41]. The following equation (2) was used:
(2)
φ c
=
φ q
(
k c
k q
)
(
η c
η q
)
2
Where the subscripts ‘‘c’’ and ‘‘q’’ refer to CQDs and quinine sulfate, respectively; φ is the quantum yield, k is the slope of the integrated emission (area under the curve of fluorescence spectra) plotted against absorbance at the excitation wavelength, and η is the refractive index of the solvent. For this, 1 mg of sample of CQDs was dissolved in 10 mL of distilled water, while 1 mg of reference (quinine sulfate) was dissolved in 100 mL of 0.1 M H2SO4 (54% quantum yield), five dilutions of the first solution were prepared (1:10,1:20,1:30,1:40,1:50), aiming to keep the absorbance below 0.10 at 360 nm. All samples were measured using a UV/Vis spectrophotometer (Thermo scientific Genesys 10 UV scanning, United Kingdom). The refractive index was measured on a refractometer (Boeco, Germany) at environmental temperature, and the integrated fluorescence emission was determined by fluorescence spectra on a spectrofluorometer (Fluoroskan II, Lab. System, Finland) at a wavelength of 380–700 nm.
The CQDs synthesized under optimal conditions from both methods were characterized to compare their properties.

The UV/Vis absorption spectrum was obtained using 3 mL of aqueous solution with a concentration of 1 mg/mL of CQDs using a spectrophotometer (Thermo scientific Genesys 10 UV scanning, United Kingdom) to a wavelength from 230 nm to 700 nm, and the baseline was measured with distilled water.

The fluorescence spectrum was obtained using CQDs in an aqueous solution at a concentration of 1 mg/mL, with an emission wavelength from 380 to 700 nm and an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. This procedure was performed with a spectrofluorometer (Fluoroskan II, Lab. System, Finland) equipped with a xenon lamp as the excitation source, and the baseline was obtained with distilled water.

Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FTIR) was done with a spectrometer (Cary 630 FTIR Spectrometer, Agilent, USA). Briefly, 10 mg of CQDs were dispersed in 1 mL of distilled water. Then, a drop of the sample was deposited on the sample-holder, and the spectra were measured in a wavenumber range from 600 to 4000 cm−1.

The nanoparticle size was measured through image analysis from HR-TEM images. For this, 1 mg of sample was dissolved in 10 mL of ethanol-water solution (1:1) and sonicated for 15 min in an ultrasonic bath (Cole-Parmer 08895–69, 40 kHz, USA). Then, five drops of the CQDs solution were dripped on a copper grid coated with an ultrathin amorphous carbon film and dried under ambient conditions. Images were obtained from a microscope (TEM, JEOLJSM7800) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV and analyzed using the “ImageJ” software (ImageJ 1.50i National Institute of Health, Bethesda, Maryland, USA) to determine diameter and interplanar spacing.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained from an X-Ray diffractometer (Empyrean, Malvern Analytical, United Kingdom). Sample preparation consisted in sonicating (Cole-Parmer 08895–69, 40 kHz, USA) 22 mg of CQDs in 2 mL of water-ethanol solution (1:1) for 15 min, then dripping it on the sample holder and drying at 30 °C to obtain the diffractogram with X-Ray detector (PIXcel 1D, Malvern Panalytical, United Kingdom) and a Cu Kα radiation source (45 Kv, 40 mA, λ = 1.5406 Å). The apparatus was programmed for a scan step size of 0.026°, time by step of 350 s, and the diffractogram was analyzed in “OriginPro 8 ®” software (OriginLab Corp., USA). The interplanar spacing was calculated by Bragg law (equation (3)):
(3)
d =
λ
2 s i n
θ
Where “d” are the interplanar spacing, “λ” is the radiation of the radiation source, and “θ” is Bragg angle.
All analyses were carried out in triplicate, and the data was reported as the mean ± the standard deviation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrothermal and microwave synthesis methods. Effect of process conditions

ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 shows that the synthesis of CQDs using the microwave methodology (MS) had the highest mass yields (31–96%) compared to conventional heating (CHS) (32–66%), These results are similar to those reported by other authors, with the microwave method generating yields between 64% and 85% [42] and yields from conventional hydrothermal synthesis ranging from 31% to 58% [41,43].

These results show that the mass yield is better by the microwave methodology, but it does not mean that the purity of sample is higher, as these values could also correspond to other compounds found in the Sargassum extract, such as carbohydrates or amino acids, that only reached intermediate synthesis steps due to short processing residence times [44–46].

As presented in Fig. 1A and B, the synthesis time and the power of the microwave oven are inversely related to the CQDs mass yield. While the yield is negatively affected in intermediate times and high temperatures.

Statistical data analysis shows that the mass yield for MS fits a linear model with a low R2 (0.4028), but adequate precision higher (>4), where time has a significant effect (P = 0.0011) and inverse behavior to the response. This could indicate that at longer times the nanocrystal growth is faster, and these substances precipitated during purification, decreasing mass concentration [37,44].

Conventional hydrothermal synthesis data were adjusted to a quadratic model (R2 = 0.6611), where time (p = 0.0030) and its interaction (p = 0.0011) with temperature had a significant effect, increasing the mass yield at longer times, and indicating that as the processing proceeds, the nuclei are likely to break during carbonization, promoting the generation of material in a smaller size that does not precipitate during centrifugation [47].

As observed in Fig. 2 the MS method generates particles with smaller QY (6–8%), than those from CHS (8–26%), and even smaller than other authors reports (5–29%) [37,42]. In the microwave method the smaller values could be related to the generation of large nuclei [46], as the fast synthesis process promotes the loss of functional groups, decreasing the QY [34,35,47].

Conventional hydrothermal method QY values were in the lower limit of those reported in literature [48] but were higher than QY values in samples obtained by MS, probably due to the differences in the origin raw materials. This could be related to the more drastic processing conditions (longer times-higher temperature and pressures) applied, in addition to there being no exchange of materials with the environment, and the reaction volume being constant, unlike the microwave process [35], causing the reaction to follow both an aromatization and a polymerization process, resulting in a higher QY [34,44,46].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CQDs synthesized at optimal conditions
ce:section.ce:para: The optimal synthesis conditions were obtained from the models generated before, where optimal response values of quantum and mass yields were fixed as target values. For microwave synthesis, these process conditions were at 400 J s−1 of oven power for 200 s, while in conventional hydrothermal synthesis, the conditions were 170 °C for 720 min.

The mass and quantum yields for the synthesis process are shown in Table 2 . The values are in the range indicated above, confirming that the mass yield is higher under microwave synthesis, while the QY is higher for CHS, fitting with results reported by other authors: Ou et al. [16] reported a mass yield of 69% using 1,3,6-trinitropyrene dissolved in dimethylformamide at 180 °C for 12 h, while Zhang et al. [42] cited yields in a range of 64–85% using citric acid, ethanolamine or tris(hydroxymethyl) aminomethane, being higher than those obtained from CHS. Regarding quantum yields, Nallayagari et al. [19] reported results in a range of 4–24% using glucosamine hydrochloride, using mixtures of N-octylamine or 1,6-diaminhexane as a nitrogen source, resulting in better yields than those obtained by microwave synthesis (QY of 0.2–10%). In their work, Kasmiarno et al. [32], using Rambutan and Pandan leaves as a carbon source, observed the same effect in the quantum yield value where conventional hydrothermal synthesis had a higher value than microwave synthesis.

CQDs synthesized using different heating methods showed dissimilar mass and quantum yields, in order to identify the cause of these differences, some properties such as their size, surface and internal bonds, as well as their phase structure were evaluated.

The images obtained by TEM (Fig. 3 ), show that particles synthesized for both methods have a spherical shape, with a slight coalescence between the particles, which is more evident in CHS than in MS samples. This effect could be due to the chemical nature of the carbon source and the concentration of intermediate products, where the source could follow a different carbonization process because of its composition. For example: carbohydrates (glucose, fucose, guluronic, and mannuronic acids, polysaccharides as fucoidan) [49] go through different processes such as hydrolysis, dehydration, decarboxylation, polymerization and finally aromatization or aggregation, while peptides (Kajalalide F) are hydrolyzed to amino acids (glutamic and aspartic acid) [7], which are subsequently polymerized, and condensed into aromatic clusters with a nitrogen substituent, and are transformed into heterocyclic structures through Maillard reactions. Chemical species generated during the intermediate steps of these reactions could contribute to the coalescence effect [15,34].

The size of CQDs is shown also in Fig. 3, where those synthetized by microwave process (Fig. 3C) have a particle size distribution in the range of 1.0–5.5 nm, with a mean diameter of 2.49 ± 0.399 nm, and those obtained via conventional hydrothermal synthesis (Fig. 3D) varied in a size from 1.0 to 5.0 nm, with a mean diameter of 2.14 ± 0.122 nm. Size results are presented in Table 3 , coinciding with the ones reported by other researchers that used carbon sources such as biomass and simple compounds.

The differences between size ranges could be due to the nanoparticle purification process. For example, Chae et al. [36] carried out sonication to resuspend the particles, this could generate a broken carbonaceous nucleus that generates particles that do no precipitate in centrifugation at 4000 rpm and they were able to pass through 3500 Da dialysis membranes; Architha et al. [22] used centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 20 min and finish the purification process with a chromatography column, while Dai et al. [50] used a centrifugation of 13,000 rpm for 16 min, after filtering with a 0.22 μm membrane. In other study, Yin et al. [21] used a 0.22 μm membrane and dialyzed with a 3500 Mw bag. The last three authors obtained a smaller range of sizes in comparison to this research where we carried out 2 centrifugation processes, first with water at 5000 rpm for 15 min, and a second with acetone at 15,000 rpm for 15 min to precipitate large particles [39]. The mean diameters of particles in all studies correspond to those reported for CQDs, between 1 and 10 nm [14,24].

The interplanar spacing of the particles was 0.21 ± 0.014 nm, and 0.21 ± 0.002 nm for microwaves and conventional heating respectively, both correspond to the graphitic plane (100). This value of interlayer spacing has been the most commonly reported in HRTEM by other authors who synthesized CQDs for both methods, using diverse carbon sources such as agaric mushrooms with carbamide, ammonium citrate dibasic, lysine, succinic acid with tris (2-aminoethyl) amine, sugar cane bagasse, cellulolytic enzyme lignin, Tagetes erecta and Mexican mint leaves, empty fruit bunch (EFB) biochar [13,21–23,30,36,37,51–53].

From HRTEM micrographs of each synthesis method (Fig. 4 ) it is possible to observe differences between crystalline order. MS images show a prevalent amorphous structure, as can be seen from the fewer amount of lines that represent the atoms in a crystalline arrangement [54], while in the CHS micrograph, the quantity of lines is higher, indicating an increase of the crystallite order [55].

These results show that for both methods, the synthesized nanoparticles have similar sizes, making this parameter not highly relevant when choosing between heating process. However, the crystallite order is different in each method, which can be a possible explanation for the changes in QY values.
The crystalline structure of CQDs was obtained by X-ray diffraction and is presented in Fig. 5 .

Fig. 5A corresponds to MS and shows three peaks: the first is a broad peak at 22.7°, with an interplanar spacing of 0.39 nm, possibly corresponding to graphitic carbon, as reported by other authors [22,24,37,56], based on its crystalline plane (002), with the maximum intensity at ∼22° [38]. Furthermore, the presence of amorphous phase of carbon is also evident by our results of HRTEM analysis, and the presence of peaks in the range of 16–20°, as reported by Sargin et al. [38] and the data of crystallographic card ICSD 00-048-1206.

The second and third peaks in MS emerge at 31.5° and 45.2°, having an interplanar spacing of 0.28 nm and 0.20 nm respectively. Both peaks have been reported as graphite carbon with (100) and (102) lattices planes [22,51], meaning that the structure is a mix amorphous and graphitic carbon.

The results for the CHS method show 6 peaks (Fig. 5B). The first one, at 22.6° with an interplanar spacing of 0.36 nm, and graphitic carbon structure [22,37,56]. The second at 28.3°, with a distance of 0.32 nm, and surrounded by other signals at 27° and 29° attributed to the nitrogen presence [53]. The next peaks at 31.6°, 40.3° and 45.4°, with interplanar distances of 0.28 nm, 0.22 nm and 0.20 nm respectively, all corresponding to graphitic carbon structure [22,51], and the last one at 56.4° with interplanar spacing of 0.16 nm, is reported with carbon diamond structure [22]. For all these peaks, lattice planes supporting their structure, (002), (100), (102), and (600) have been reported [22,37,51].

Comparing both synthesis methods diffractograms, it is observed that the CHS method shows higher intensities in the peaks at 28.27°, 40.43° and 56.35° (2θ°). Furthermore, the shape changes of 22.55° signal and the higher intensity of 40.43°, 45.32° and 56.35° peaks with respect to MS indicate the development of a higher crystallinity. While the increase in the intensity of the 28.27° peak indicates a better nitrogen doping related to a structural defect that causes an increase in quantum yield, which is in accordance with our fluorescence spectra results. Also, the crystallinity structure observed in HRTEM supports our diffractogram results.

FTIR spectroscopy is based on the interaction between the bonds and photons in the infrared region, which promotes the transitions between rotational and vibrational energy levels of the ground electronic energy state, these vibration bands can be correlated to single bonds, and the functional groups of the molecule [57,58].

Fig. 6 A and B shows the FTIR spectra of the synthesized particles for both methods, where they show peaks in similar positions. They have a broad peak at 3260 cm−1 that is characteristic for –OH/–NH bonds that is characteristic for –OH/–NH bonds [24,25,35,46,50,52]; the well-defined peak at 1637 cm−1, could be assigned to CO bonds for carboxyl or amide I, because the carbon source used for the synthesis were carbohydrates and proteins. According to other authors, these functional groups are detected in the range of 1633–1660 cm−1 [19,35–37,55,58]. Another peak is observed at 626 cm−1, and it was reported to correspond to aromatic compounds with sp2 hybridizations [22]. Additional small peaks at 1373,1238 and 1045 cm−1 can be observed: the first value corresponds to the C–O bond [13,26], CN of amide III [47,52] or CH2 [38], the second indicates the presence of C–N [38,47], CN [47], C–O [15,52] or C–C [59], and the last one could be for C–O–C [50,59] or C–O from alkoxy [22,26,56].

The UV/Vis spectra give information about the electronic transitions occurring in the material, which are between π-bonds, σ-bonds and lone electron pairs (n-transition) [58]. The differences between FTIR spectra and this analysis are in relation to the transitions detected, while the IR radiation causes bond vibrations and rotations, the UV/Vis radiation causes changes in the electronic levels [57].

Fig. 7 shows the UV/Vis spectra for each method, where in both spectra the pronounced peak at 286 nm for microwave and 290 nm for conventional hydrothermal synthesis stands out. This could correspond to a π→π* transition of CC aromatic structures with sp2 hybridization [21,22,32,52], furthermore, Yan et al. [60] obtained this peak at 289 nm assigning it the indole structure. Fig. 7B, that corresponds to CQDs synthesized by conventional heating, shows deformations in the spectra, with shoulders appearing at wavelengths of 329 and 365 nm; these were reported for n→π* transition of functional groups [21,32]; Prekodravac et al. [61] assign the C–N/CN bonds to a point close to 320 nm, while Saikia et al. [17] and Yan et al. [60] indicate that the peaks in the 370 nm region are for CO binding. For microwave synthesis a wide elongation can be observed on the right side of the spectrum, indicating that there are masked peaks in the region of 300–400 nm, which as mentioned previously could be the n→π* transition of functional groups C–N/CN/CO [21].

The differences between the two spectra are notorious, indicating that the type of thermal treatment applied affects the CQDs atomic structure. These results could be related with the nitrogen and oxygen doping in the nanoparticle nucleus [18], where the CHS sample had a better inclusion of atoms, inducing higher structural defects that improve the quantum confinement [16,18], increasing the QY as a result of the n→π* transitions, detected at 300–400 nm absorbance range, and where the analyzed structure has a lone pair of electrons that are not detected in FTIR spectra.

Fig. 8 shows the fluorescence spectra with the excitation wavelength of 360 nm at the same concentration (absorbance = 0.044), where the particles produced by CHS are more luminescent than those produced by MS, with the highest fluorescence intensity of CQDs for both methods being observed between 380 and 540 nm, corresponding to the violet, blue, and green colors. In addition, the peak is at 431 nm for conventional heating and 437.8 nm for microwave. Other authors reported similar maximum values; Sargin et al. [38] with an excitation of 345 nm and a maximum emission at 427 nm, Architha et al. [22] excited at 320–380 nm, and emitted at 436 nm, while Wang et al. [11] reported an λEx = 340–360 nm and λEm = 429 nm.

The CQDs from pelagic Sargassum as a carbon source were successfully synthesized by microwave and conventional hydrothermal methods, obtaining the optimal conditions for each process where MS has the better mass yield, and CHS shows the best QY value. The size and surface functional groups are similar for both methods; however, the crystalline structure and core doping by nitrogen and oxygen show the best result in CHS, which correlates with the results of QY measurements. The application of these nanoparticles can be related to their fluorescence properties: CQDs obtained by the CHS method would be better suited for future use for bioimaging or as fluorescence sensors; while nanoparticles produced by MS would be helpful as drug and gene carriers, due to the higher concentration obtained by this method, the shorter reaction times that lead to lower energy costs, and the chemical surface composition.

Héctor U. Castañeda-Serna: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Visualization. Georgina Calderón-Domínguez: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Resources, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Arturo García-Bórquez: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Resources, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Ma de la Paz Salgado-Cruz: Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Reynold R. Farrera Rebollo: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Resources, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Héctor Uriel Castañeda Serna thanks CONACyT and SIP-IPN for the scholarships provided and the financial support for this work. This research was funded through the projects: 20195500, 20201679, 20210624, 20211381 and 20210918 from the Instituto Politécnico Nacional (IPN, México) and 1668 from CONACyT (México).
